text: Active catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are needed for solar driven hydrogen evolution photoelectrochemical devices using water as the sacrificial resource. We have determined the cause for an order of magnitude increase in OER activity for mixed (Ni,Fe)oxyhydroxides—500 times greater than that of their pure parent compounds—making this catalyst one of the most active ever reported in alkaline solution. Using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) at the Ni K and Fe K edges collected with high energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) on catalyst electrodes that are acting during OER and also after several hours of operation we have elucidated the chemical properties of Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH catalysts that are responsible for their marked improvements over those of the parent pure metal compounds. Our data reveal that the Fe3+ ion resides at the octahedral site of Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH and has shortened Fe-O bonds relative to those of homogeneous FeOOH which is a consequence of the Ni-oxide shared edge with an adjacent [NiO6] site. Modeling of simulation studies of OER for this mixed metal catalyst, we confirm the edge-sharing between the iron and nickel sites in Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH, leads to a nearly optimal adsorption energy of OER-reactions intermediates at the iron centers along with low overpotentials. Additionally, we found that nickel in Ni(1-x)Fe(x)OOH does not act as an active site for OER.
